title: Haemochromatosis
oneLiner: Hereditary iron overload (most common in Northern Europeans, HFE mutation) causing progressive organ damage (liver, heart, pancreas, joints) – treatable with venesection if caught early.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Iron cardiomyopathy (arrhythmia / acute heart failure):** ECG showing new atrial fibrillation, heart block, or ventricular ectopy + raised JVP/bibasal crackles → treat as acute decompensated HF (do NOT discharge with “just fatigue”)
- **Variceal haemorrhage (if cirrhosis present):** haematemesis/melena with hypotension → urgent OGD and resuscitation (do not assume it’s just iron deficiency)
- **Hypoglycaemic coma (due to diabetes from pancreatic iron deposition):** check capillary glucose in any drowsy patient with known haemochromatosis
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fatigue / lethargy (most common)
  - Arthralgia (especially MCP joints 2&3, knees, hips) – often labelled osteoarthritis
  - Abdominal pain / hepatomegaly
  - Loss of libido / impotence (hypogonadism)
  - Diabetes mellitus (type 1-like, but secondary)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Bronze/grey skin pigmentation** (especially sun-exposed areas)
  - Hepatomegaly (may be tender)
  - Signs of cirrhosis (spider naevi, caput medusae, ascites)
  - Loss of body hair / testicular atrophy
  - Arthritis / loss of hand grip
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely with 1-line differentiators**
  - **Alcoholic liver disease / NASH (non‑alcoholic steatohepatitis):** iron studies show raised ferritin but **transferrin saturation <45%** (vs >45% in haemochromatosis) – splitter comes below
  - **Secondary iron overload (transfusion‑dependent anaemias, myelodysplasia, chronic haemolysis):** history of blood transfusions + multiple blood transfusions + low hepcidin; transferrin saturation often high but ferritin disproportionately high
  - **Porphyria cutanea tarda:** photosensitivity + blistering + hypertrichosis + raised ferritin; **check porphyrins in urine**
  - **Hepatocellular carcinoma (de novo or in cirrhosis):** imaging (+ fétoprotein) often needed; may co‑exist with haemochromatosis
- **Splitter:** **Transferrin saturation >45% (especially >60%)** distinguishes hereditary haemochromatosis from other causes of hyperferritinaemia
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick (glucose if new diabetes)
  - ECG (look for arrhythmia)
  - Capillary glucose (if drowsy)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Iron studies:** serum iron, TIBC, **transferrin saturation** (calculate or requested as %) – **>45% is suggestive, >60% strongly so** (in men; >50% in women)
  - **Serum ferritin:** typically >300 µg/L (men) or >200 µg/L (women); can be normal in early stages
  - **LFTs, U&Es, creatinine, FBC, clotting**
  - **HFE genotyping** (C282Y, H63D mutations) – diagnostic; C282Y homozygosity most common
  - **HbA1c** (screening for diabetes) – but interpret carefully if iron overload affects red cell turnover
  - **Hepatitis B, C** (to rule out co‑existing viral hepatitis)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** liver ultrasound (look for steatosis, cirrhosis, HCC)
  - **Second-line:** MRI T2* (quantitative liver iron concentration) – non‑invasive and avoids biopsy; also cardiac MRI if suspected cardiomyopathy
- **Special tests:**
  - **Liver biopsy** – reserved for cases where non‑invasive imaging is inconclusive or when cirrhosis severity needs quantification (Metavir fibrosis stage); **not routine if genotyping is positive and clinical picture clear**
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **No single scoring system** routinely used in UK; diagnosis based on **transferrin saturation >45% + ferritin > upper limit + HFE mutation** (homozygous C282Y) plus evidence of iron overload on MRI/liver biopsy
  - **NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary** guides: refer to gastroenterology/hepatology if TS >45% or ferritin >500 µg/L + high suspicion
- **Never-miss test:** **Transferrin saturation** and **HFE genotyping** – missing these delays diagnosis while cirrhosis develops (can be prevented)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute decompensation (arrhythmia, heart failure, variceal bleeding, hypoglycaemia) – manage accordingly (see Killers First)
  - **Do not start venesection** until iron overload confirmed with serum markers and genotyping (unless urgent indication for chelation in acute iron toxicity – but that’s rare)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis: iron studies, genotyping, liver imaging
  - **Start venesection** once diagnosis established (typically 200-500 mL weekly, according to local blood bank protocol)
  - **Monitor full blood count** before each venesection (Hb >100 g/L to proceed)
  - **Check ferritin** monthly initially to guide frequency; target ferritin **<50-100 µg/L**
  - **Screen for complications:** diabetes (fasting glucose, HbA1c), hypogonadism (testosterone level), cardiac involvement (echocardiogram if arrhythmia or heart failure)
  - **Give dietary advice:** avoid alcohol (accelerates liver fibrosis), avoid iron supplements (including multivitamins with iron), avoid vitamin C megadoses (enhances iron absorption – limit to <200 mg/day)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance venesection every 2-4 months (or as needed) to keep ferritin <50 µg/L (some units <100)
  - If venesection not possible (anaemia, venous access, patient refusal) – **iron chelation** (deferasirox, deferoxamine) but rarely first‑line in UK
  - **Surveillance:** 6‑12 monthly LFTs, ferritin, glucose, liver ultrasound (for HCC, if cirrhosis present)
  - **Vaccinate:** hepatitis A and B (prevents further liver injury)
  - **Lifestyle:** weight reduction if NAFLD co‑exists; statin if hyperlipidaemia (some evidence reduces HCC risk in cirrhosis)
- **Default Orders (if admitted for venesection):**
  - **Fluids:** no specific need unless bleeding; intravenous iron is contraindicated
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** yes (if age >18 and not bleeding, unless low risk)
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet for venesection (drink 500 mL water before phlebotomy to avoid vasovagal)
  - **Observation:** pre‑ and post‑venesection BP, pulse; watch for vasovagal
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent (venesection):** no drug – it’s a procedure. Write: “Venesection 200-500 mL weekly (according to local blood bank policy).” No dose guess.
- **Alternatives (if venesection not tolerated):**
  - **Deferasirox (Exjade®)** (oral) – dose: [check local policy – typically 10-20 mg/kg once daily, adjust per response/toxicity]
  - **Deferoxamine (Desferal®)** (subcutaneous/IV) – dose: [check local policy – typically 20-40 mg/kg/day over 8-12 hours via pump]
  - **Deferiprone (Ferriprox®)** (oral) – dose: [check local policy – usually 25 mg/kg three times daily]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Vitamin C:** high doses >200 mg/day can cause rapid iron mobilisation → cardiac toxicity; avoid during chelation therapy
  - **Iron supplements:** absolute contraindication until iron deficiency (rare in treated haemochromatosis)
  - **Statins:** some data suggest benefit in cirrhosis; no significant interaction
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Deferasirox:** adjust dose in mild‑moderate hepatic impairment; avoid if severe (Child‑Pugh C)
  - **Deferoxamine:** contraindicated in severe renal failure (creatinine clearance <20 mL/min) – [check local policy]
  - **Deferiprone:** avoid in hepatic impairment; reduce dose if creatinine >2 × ULN
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Venesection too frequent** causing iron deficiency anaemia (Hb <100 g/L) → stop venesection, check ferritin. If ferritin already low (<50) with low Hb, the patient is now iron deficient – do not continue venesection.
  - **Deferoxamine at high dose** in patient with fast iron mobilisation → can cause acute cardiac toxicity (arrhythmia, hypotension) – check ECG before infusion
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (AF, heart block, ventricular tachycardia)
  - Acute decompensated heart failure (restrictive cardiomyopathy)
  - Variceal haemorrhage (if cirrhosis)
  - Vasovagal syncope during venesection
- **Chronic:**
  - **Liver:** cirrhosis (20-30% of untreated homozygotes), hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – risk remains even after iron removal
  - **Heart:** dilated cardiomyopathy, conduction defects, arrhythmia
  - **Pancreas:** diabetes mellitus (type 2 but brittle)
  - **Joints:** osteoarthritis (especially MCP 2&3, knees, hips) – often irreversible
  - **Endocrine:** hypogonadism (loss of libido, osteoporosis, infertility), hypothyroidism
  - **Skin:** hyperpigmentation
  - **Infection:** increased risk of Yersinia enterocolitica (iron rich environment favours growth) – avoid raw seafood
sections.Prognosis:
- **Excellent if diagnosed early** (pre‑cirrhosis, pre‑diabetes) – normal life expectancy with venesection
- **Once cirrhosis is present** (liver biopsy F3‑F4) – 5‑year survival ~60% even with iron removal; HCC risk remains (requires surveillance)
- **Cardiac involvement** carries high mortality if untreated, but reversible with intensive venesection/chelation
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check transferrin saturation** in any patient with unexplained fatigue, arthralgia, or abnormal LFTs – it’s cheap and non‑invasive
- **Do HFE genotyping** in anyone with TS >45% or ferritin >500 µg/L + clinical suspicion
- **Screen family members** (first‑degree relatives) for HFE mutations and iron studies – early detection prevents organ damage
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start venesection** empirically without genotyping and excluding secondary overload (could cause anaemia in transfusional siderosis)
- **Do NOT ** stop venesection after ferritin normalises** – maintenance is key to prevent re‑accumulation
- **Do NOT** forget to **screen for HCC** (6‑monthly ultrasound) in patients with cirrhosis – even after iron depletion, HCC risk persists
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - New chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath (cardiac involvement)
  - Vomiting blood or black stools (variceal bleed)
  - Confusion or lethargy (hypoglycaemia or hepatic encephalopathy)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Gastroenterology/hepatology clinic (or haematology if venesection performed by blood bank) – typically seen in 2‑4 weeks for first review
  - Genetic counselling for family screening – offer to index patient and provide contact for local clinical genetics service
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or any red‑flag deterioration
  - Acute arrhythmia causing hypotension (SBP <90) or loss of consciousness
  - Suspected variceal haemorrhage (call upper GI bleed team)
  - Acute confusion/coma (check glucose – if <4 mmol/L call crash team; if >15+ and ketones, suspect DKA)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (suggests tissue hypoperfusion from cardiac failure/bleed)
  - **GCS <13** or new neurological deficit (rule out hepatic encephalopathy or hypoglycaemia)
  - **ECG: new AF with rapid ventricular response** >140 bpm not controlled by IV beta‑blocker → call cardiology/ICU

rawMarkdown:
# Haemochromatosis

**One-liner:** Hereditary iron overload (most common in Northern Europeans, HFE mutation) causing progressive organ damage (liver, heart, pancreas, joints) – treatable with venesection if caught early.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Iron cardiomyopathy (arrhythmia / acute heart failure):** ECG showing new atrial fibrillation, heart block, or ventricular ectopy + raised JVP/bibasal crackles → treat as acute decompensated HF (do NOT discharge with “just fatigue”)
- **Variceal haemorrhage (if cirrhosis present):** haematemesis/melena with hypotension → urgent OGD and resuscitation (do not assume it’s just iron deficiency)
- **Hypoglycaemic coma (due to diabetes from pancreatic iron deposition):** check capillary glucose in any drowsy patient with known haemochromatosis

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fatigue / lethargy (most common)
  - Arthralgia (especially MCP joints 2&3, knees, hips) – often labelled osteoarthritis
  - Abdominal pain / hepatomegaly
  - Loss of libido / impotence (hypogonadism)
  - Diabetes mellitus (type 1-like, but secondary)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Bronze/grey skin pigmentation** (especially sun-exposed areas)
  - Hepatomegaly (may be tender)
  - Signs of cirrhosis (spider naevi, caput medusae, ascites)
  - Loss of body hair / testicular atrophy
  - Arthritis / loss of hand grip

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely with 1-line differentiators**
  - **Alcoholic liver disease / NASH (non‑alcoholic steatohepatitis):** iron studies show raised ferritin but **transferrin saturation <45%** (vs >45% in haemochromatosis) – splitter comes below
  - **Secondary iron overload (transfusion‑dependent anaemias, myelodysplasia, chronic haemolysis):** history of blood transfusions + multiple blood transfusions + low hepcidin; transferrin saturation often high but ferritin disproportionately high
  - **Porphyria cutanea tarda:** photosensitivity + blistering + hypertrichosis + raised ferritin; **check porphyrins in urine**
  - **Hepatocellular carcinoma (de novo or in cirrhosis):** imaging (+ fétoprotein) often needed; may co‑exist with haemochromatosis
- **Splitter:** **Transferrin saturation >45% (especially >60%)** distinguishes hereditary haemochromatosis from other causes of hyperferritinaemia

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick (glucose if new diabetes)
  - ECG (look for arrhythmia)
  - Capillary glucose (if drowsy)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Iron studies:** serum iron, TIBC, **transferrin saturation** (calculate or requested as %) – **>45% is suggestive, >60% strongly so** (in men; >50% in women)
  - **Serum ferritin:** typically >300 µg/L (men) or >200 µg/L (women); can be normal in early stages
  - **LFTs, U&Es, creatinine, FBC, clotting**
  - **HFE genotyping** (C282Y, H63D mutations) – diagnostic; C282Y homozygosity most common
  - **HbA1c** (screening for diabetes) – but interpret carefully if iron overload affects red cell turnover
  - **Hepatitis B, C** (to rule out co‑existing viral hepatitis)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** liver ultrasound (look for steatosis, cirrhosis, HCC)
  - **Second-line:** MRI T2* (quantitative liver iron concentration) – non‑invasive and avoids biopsy; also cardiac MRI if suspected cardiomyopathy
- **Special tests:**
  - **Liver biopsy** – reserved for cases where non‑invasive imaging is inconclusive or when cirrhosis severity needs quantification (Metavir fibrosis stage); **not routine if genotyping is positive and clinical picture clear**
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **No single scoring system** routinely used in UK; diagnosis based on **transferrin saturation >45% + ferritin > upper limit + HFE mutation** (homozygous C282Y) plus evidence of iron overload on MRI/liver biopsy
  - **NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary** guides: refer to gastroenterology/hepatology if TS >45% or ferritin >500 µg/L + high suspicion
- **Never-miss test:** **Transferrin saturation** and **HFE genotyping** – missing these delays diagnosis while cirrhosis develops (can be prevented)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute decompensation (arrhythmia, heart failure, variceal bleeding, hypoglycaemia) – manage accordingly (see Killers First)
  - **Do not start venesection** until iron overload confirmed with serum markers and genotyping (unless urgent indication for chelation in acute iron toxicity – but that’s rare)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis: iron studies, genotyping, liver imaging
  - **Start venesection** once diagnosis established (typically 200-500 mL weekly, according to local blood bank protocol)
  - **Monitor full blood count** before each venesection (Hb >100 g/L to proceed)
  - **Check ferritin** monthly initially to guide frequency; target ferritin **<50-100 µg/L**
  - **Screen for complications:** diabetes (fasting glucose, HbA1c), hypogonadism (testosterone level), cardiac involvement (echocardiogram if arrhythmia or heart failure)
  - **Give dietary advice:** avoid alcohol (accelerates liver fibrosis), avoid iron supplements (including multivitamins with iron), avoid vitamin C megadoses (enhances iron absorption – limit to <200 mg/day)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance venesection every 2-4 months (or as needed) to keep ferritin <50 µg/L (some units <100)
  - If venesection not possible (anaemia, venous access, patient refusal) – **iron chelation** (deferasirox, deferoxamine) but rarely first‑line in UK
  - **Surveillance:** 6‑12 monthly LFTs, ferritin, glucose, liver ultrasound (for HCC, if cirrhosis present)
  - **Vaccinate:** hepatitis A and B (prevents further liver injury)
  - **Lifestyle:** weight reduction if NAFLD co‑exists; statin if hyperlipidaemia (some evidence reduces HCC risk in cirrhosis)
- **Default Orders (if admitted for venesection):**
  - **Fluids:** no specific need unless bleeding; intravenous iron is contraindicated
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** yes (if age >18 and not bleeding, unless low risk)
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet for venesection (drink 500 mL water before phlebotomy to avoid vasovagal)
  - **Observation:** pre‑ and post‑venesection BP, pulse; watch for vasovagal

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent (venesection):** no drug – it’s a procedure. Write: “Venesection 200-500 mL weekly (according to local blood bank policy).” No dose guess.
- **Alternatives (if venesection not tolerated):**
  - **Deferasirox (Exjade®)** (oral) – dose: [check local policy – typically 10-20 mg/kg once daily, adjust per response/toxicity]
  - **Deferoxamine (Desferal®)** (subcutaneous/IV) – dose: [check local policy – typically 20-40 mg/kg/day over 8-12 hours via pump]
  - **Deferiprone (Ferriprox®)** (oral) – dose: [check local policy – usually 25 mg/kg three times daily]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Vitamin C:** high doses >200 mg/day can cause rapid iron mobilisation → cardiac toxicity; avoid during chelation therapy
  - **Iron supplements:** absolute contraindication until iron deficiency (rare in treated haemochromatosis)
  - **Statins:** some data suggest benefit in cirrhosis; no significant interaction
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Deferasirox:** adjust dose in mild‑moderate hepatic impairment; avoid if severe (Child‑Pugh C)
  - **Deferoxamine:** contraindicated in severe renal failure (creatinine clearance <20 mL/min) – [check local policy]
  - **Deferiprone:** avoid in hepatic impairment; reduce dose if creatinine >2 × ULN
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Venesection too frequent** causing iron deficiency anaemia (Hb <100 g/L) → stop venesection, check ferritin. If ferritin already low (<50) with low Hb, the patient is now iron deficient – do not continue venesection.
  - **Deferoxamine at high dose** in patient with fast iron mobilisation → can cause acute cardiac toxicity (arrhythmia, hypotension) – check ECG before infusion

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (AF, heart block, ventricular tachycardia)
  - Acute decompensated heart failure (restrictive cardiomyopathy)
  - Variceal haemorrhage (if cirrhosis)
  - Vasovagal syncope during venesection
- **Chronic:**
  - **Liver:** cirrhosis (20-30% of untreated homozygotes), hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – risk remains even after iron removal
  - **Heart:** dilated cardiomyopathy, conduction defects, arrhythmia
  - **Pancreas:** diabetes mellitus (type 2 but brittle)
  - **Joints:** osteoarthritis (especially MCP 2&3, knees, hips) – often irreversible
  - **Endocrine:** hypogonadism (loss of libido, osteoporosis, infertility), hypothyroidism
  - **Skin:** hyperpigmentation
  - **Infection:** increased risk of Yersinia enterocolitica (iron rich environment favours growth) – avoid raw seafood

### Prognosis
- **Excellent if diagnosed early** (pre‑cirrhosis, pre‑diabetes) – normal life expectancy with venesection
- **Once cirrhosis is present** (liver biopsy F3‑F4) – 5‑year survival ~60% even with iron removal; HCC risk remains (requires surveillance)
- **Cardiac involvement** carries high mortality if untreated, but reversible with intensive venesection/chelation

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check transferrin saturation** in any patient with unexplained fatigue, arthralgia, or abnormal LFTs – it’s cheap and non‑invasive
- **Do HFE genotyping** in anyone with TS >45% or ferritin >500 µg/L + clinical suspicion
- **Screen family members** (first‑degree relatives) for HFE mutations and iron studies – early detection prevents organ damage

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT start venesection** empirically without genotyping and excluding secondary overload (could cause anaemia in transfusional siderosis)
- **Do NOT ** stop venesection after ferritin normalises** – maintenance is key to prevent re‑accumulation
- **Do NOT** forget to **screen for HCC** (6‑monthly ultrasound) in patients with cirrhosis – even after iron depletion, HCC risk persists

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - New chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath (cardiac involvement)
  - Vomiting blood or black stools (variceal bleed)
  - Confusion or lethargy (hypoglycaemia or hepatic encephalopathy)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Gastroenterology/hepatology clinic (or haematology if venesection performed by blood bank) – typically seen in 2‑4 weeks for first review
  - Genetic counselling for family screening – offer to index patient and provide contact for local clinical genetics service

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or any red‑flag deterioration
  - Acute arrhythmia causing hypotension (SBP <90) or loss of consciousness
  - Suspected variceal haemorrhage (call upper GI bleed team)
  - Acute confusion/coma (check glucose – if <4 mmol/L call crash team; if >15+ and ketones, suspect DKA)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (suggests tissue hypoperfusion from cardiac failure/bleed)
  - **GCS <13** or new neurological deficit (rule out hepatic encephalopathy or hypoglycaemia)
  - **ECG: new AF with rapid ventricular response** >140 bpm not controlled by IV beta‑blocker → call cardiology/ICU
